summary: Jeff Atwood questions whether the jump from DVD to HD-DVD/Blu-Ray resolution justifies the cost of new players, TVs, and media. He presents visual comparisons and cites David Pogue's experiment showing most people couldn't distinguish between 5 and 13 megapixel prints. While acknowledging that higher resolution sources do scale better even to lower-resolution displays, he argues the practical difference is highly subjective and represents diminishing returns compared to the earlier leap from standard TV to DVD. The format war, copy protection issues, and exploitation-focused business models further undermine the value proposition.
key_points: The resolution jump from 720x480 to 1920x1080 represents nearly 5x more pixels, but practical perceptibility is questionable at typical viewing distances
David Pogue's experiment showed 95% of people couldn't distinguish between 5MP and 13MP prints even up close, suggesting resolution differences are overrated
YouTube's success with 'appalling' video quality proves different rules apply to motion video than static images
Higher resolution sources provide benefits through supersampling even when displayed on lower-resolution screens
The format war, DRM pitfalls, and exploitative business practices make early adoption risky
money_quotes: How, exactly, do we explain the runaway success of YouTube, where video quality is never less than appalling? Clearly, the rules are different for images in motion.
I'm telling you, there was NO DIFFERENCE.
The practical difference between DVD resolution and next-gen DVD resolution is highly subjective.
Is anyone actually arguing that DVDs don't look good enough?
Although I'll always be a fan of increased resolution, I'm not sure these additional pixels have earned my money yet.
I also think people tend to overestimate how much detail is needed for an image of a given size.
key_insight: Resolution improvements face diminishing perceptual returns, and the technology industry often overestimates how much consumers can actually see—or care about—increased pixel density.
